text: The video demonstrates the construction of an S pouch for redo pouch surgery, highlighting its ability to extend further into the pelvis compared to a J pouch. The S pouch is formed using three limbs of terminal ileum, with seromuscular sutures approximating the ileal segments. An S-shaped enterotomy line is marked with a bovie, and stay sutures maintain orientation. Linear enterotomies are made with a tonsil or gloved finger, and 3-0 braided absorbable sutures are used for the posterior anastomotic lines. The anterior wall is closed with continuous seromuscular sutures, followed by an insufflation test with saline to check staple line integrity.

The surgeon opens the apex of the proposed J pouch with sharp dissection to extend the afferent limb further into the pelvis. The distal ileum is stapled at both ends, and the staple lines are over-sewn with continuous absorbable suture. The isolated ileal segment remains in mesenteric continuity and is aligned in an isoperistaltic orientation. Enterotomies are made in each limb, and two linear staple fires are performed, one cephalad and one caudal, on the anti-mesenteric border. The right limb of the H pouch is stapled off with a TA stapler, creating a common channel for the iliopouch anal anastomosis. Stay sutures are placed on the corners of the common enterotomy, which is closed with a continuous running seromuscular suture. An insufflation test is conducted to ensure staple line integrity.

In conclusion, although a J pouch is most commonly constructed, an S or H pouch may be necessary for some patients to avoid a permanent stoma, as sometimes a J pouch is met with anatomic or technical difficulties. S and H pouch construction must be in the armamentarium of anyone performing index or redo pouch procedures.
